title: Time Synchronization and Clock Skew for Audit Timestamps

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST 800-53 AU-8 ‚Äî Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 AU-8 (Time stamps) is enforced ‚Äî Splunk UC-22.14.7: Time Synchronization and Clock Skew for Audit Timestamps.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.7' running on WinEventLog:System (W32Time), linux:secure / ntp logs, _indextime vs _time delta, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted ‚Äî SME review required.

app: Splunk Universal Forwarder, Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742)
spl:
index=os earliest=-24h
| eval skew_sec=abs(_indextime-_time)
| stats perc95(skew_sec) as p95_skew, max(skew_sec) as max_skew by host, sourcetype
| where p95_skew>120 OR max_skew>300
description: Surfaces hosts whose event `_time` diverges from indexer time beyond tolerance, evidencing AU-8 time stamp synchronization.
value: Surfaces hosts whose event `_time` diverges from indexer time beyond tolerance, evidencing AU-8 time stamp synchronization.
implementation: (1) Standardize NTP/chrony on all forwarders; (2) tune thresholds per sourcetype (batch logs vs real-time); (3) alert Tier0 assets on breach; (4) attach remediation runbook for clock drift.
visualization: Table (worst skew by host), Single value (hosts breaching threshold), Map (optional geo context)
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes by Processes.dest | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: Surfaces hosts whose event diverges from indexer time beyond tolerance, evidencing AU-8 time stamp synchronization ‚Äî so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Universal Forwarder, Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `WinEventLog:System` (W32Time), `linux:secure` / `ntp` logs, `_indextime` vs `_time` delta.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
(1) Standardize NTP/chrony on all forwarders; (2) tune thresholds per sourcetype (batch logs vs real-time); (3) alert Tier0 assets on breach; (4) attach remediation runbook for clock drift.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os earliest=-24h
| eval skew_sec=abs(_indextime-_time)
| stats perc95(skew_sec) as p95_skew, max(skew_sec) as max_skew by host, sourcetype
| where p95_skew>120 OR max_skew>300
```

Understanding this SPL

**Time Synchronization and Clock Skew for Audit Timestamps (AU-8)** ‚Äî Surfaces hosts whose event `_time` diverges from indexer time beyond tolerance, evidencing AU-8 time stamp synchronization.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:System` (W32Time), `linux:secure` / `ntp` logs, `_indextime` vs `_time` delta. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Universal Forwarder, Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=os, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **skew_sec** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, sourcetype** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where p95_skew>120 OR max_skew>300` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes by Processes.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Time Synchronization and Clock Skew for Audit Timestamps (AU-8)** ‚Äî Surfaces hosts whose event `_time` diverges from indexer time beyond tolerance, evidencing AU-8 time stamp synchronization.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:System` (W32Time), `linux:secure` / `ntp` logs, `_indextime` vs `_time` delta. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Universal Forwarder, Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Endpoint.Processes` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (worst skew by host), Single value (hosts breaching threshold), Map (optional geo context)
